steps.title: Identify the claim
steps.content: The passage argues that it is too object-focused to judge Stevens's preserved sandpaintings only as static objects. The claim is that Stevens still conveyed ikaah as an ephemeral, dynamic cultural practice. Strong support should therefore emphasize Stevens's actions, performance, and transmission of knowledge, not merely the preserved sandpaintings themselves.

steps.title: Compare the quotations
steps.content:
• Choice A - "Stevens's ambassadorial sandpaintings are best understood not as self-contained objects but as reminders of the public creations of the sandpaintings, during which Stevens conducted appropriate ikaah rituals and encouraged viewers to closely track his movements and subtle shifts in the sand throughout the process.": this supports process, but it focuses on creation rather than the later ambassadorial act of giving.
  • Choice B - "While Stevens's ambassadorial sandpaintings are undoubtedly educative for audiences that have little exposure to Dine culture, they should not be confused with authentic ikaah, which cannot be extricated from a practice that is intentionally and necessarily transitory nor condensed into a single persistent object.": this opposes the passage's defense of Stevens.

steps.title: Reject the outside explanation
steps.content: • Choice C - "The most compelling way to reconcile the apparent tension between the temporary and performative nature of ikaah and the persistent and static nature of Stevens's ambassadorial sandpaintings is to recognize that Stevens was an ambassador not only of Dine culture but of a US art culture that tended to value permanent works over ephemeral ones.": this introduces a US art-culture explanation the passage does not give.

steps.title: Choose the action-based support
steps.content:
• Choice D - "It is important to recall Stevens's own actions as a gift-giver-a temporary and performative role-of the sandpaintings, for by taking those actions, Stevens publicly enacted the transmission of knowledge of both traditional ikaah practices and cutting-edge chemistry (in the form of the preservative additives), a hybrid that reflects the dynamism of Dine culture.": this directly supports the claim by stressing Stevens's performative role, the transmission of knowledge, and the dynamism of the practice.
The answer is D.
